policy_statement: Strengthen climate change mitigation and circular economy skills in school curricula and schoolwork and expand teachers' knowledge (2017–2021).
mechanism_description: It will strengthen climate change mitigation and circular economy skills in school curricula and schoolwork and expand teachers’ knowledge, building capacity that supports future emissions-reducing choices and practices.
primary_causal_pathway: Education & capacity building → behavior/institutional practice change → ↓ energy/material demand & ↑ uptake of low-carbon options → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Integrate climate mitigation and circular economy content into school curricula and teacher training, improving competencies across students, educators, and school institutions.  
2) Mediating process: Increased climate literacy, skills, and pro-climate social norms improve decision-making and organizational capability, which accelerates adoption of low-carbon behaviors (e.g., consumption choices, waste prevention), practices (e.g., resource efficiency), and support for mitigation policies/technologies.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced lifecycle emissions through lower energy use and material throughput and increased implementation of emissions-reducing measures across sectors over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling sustained, broad-based reductions in GHG emissions over time by increasing knowledge, skills, and norms that drive lower-carbon decisions and institutional practices.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education and skills-building increase capacity and shift behaviors/institutions toward lower-carbon choices, leading to reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “circular economy” implies resource efficiency and education can also support adaptation awareness, the mechanism’s largest climate-relevant effect is downstream emissions reduction at scale (mitigation), not immediate hazard-risk reduction or ecosystem carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the education content were explicitly focused on disaster preparedness/heat-health response, the dominant pathway could become adaptation; if focused purely on reducing material inputs without explicit emissions framing it could lean resource efficiency. In this canonical mechanism, the intended outcome is “emissions-reducing choices and practices,” supporting mitigation as primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is climate education/skills building to increase institutional/behavioral capacity for emissions reduction fits mitigation as the primary category because the intended dominant impact is lowering emissions via informed action.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any finer-grained subtype (e.g., “behavior change,” “capacity building,” “circular economy education”) should be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific program design and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Education policies often have diffuse, long-lag impacts and may also support adaptation literacy; classification hinges on the stated target (emissions-reducing choices/practices). If implemented mainly as waste-prevention or resource-reduction training without explicit emissions goals, some instances could be argued as Resource Efficiency, but the canonical mechanism explicitly points to emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “skills related to the mitigation of climate change and to circular economy will be strengthened in school curricula and in schoolwork”; “Teachers’ knowledge will be expanded”
co_benefits: Improved general education outcomes and workforce skills; reduced waste and resource use beyond climate benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a regulatory curriculum mandate if tied to binding national curriculum requirements; the text does not specify enforceable standards, so classified as planning/strategy (education & capacity building).
